questions.question: What is a 'dangling pointer'?
questions.hint: A pointer to memory that has been freed.

questions.question: What is 'lvalue'?
questions.hint: An expression that can be on the left side of an assignment.

questions.question: What is an 'opaque pointer'?
questions.hint: A pointer to an incomplete structure type.

questions.question: What is 'sequence point'?
questions.hint: Point where all side effects of previous evaluations are complete.

questions.question: What is 'pointer decay'?
questions.hint: Array conversion to a pointer to its first element.

questions.question: What is 'short-circuit evaluation'?
questions.hint: Skipping evaluation of the second operand in logical expressions.

questions.question: What does 'volatile' do?
questions.hint: Tells compiler the value can change unexpectedly.

questions.question: What is 'undefined behavior'?
questions.hint: Result of code that the C standard doesn't specify.

questions.question: What is 'heap fragmentation'?
questions.hint: Small holes in memory after many allocations/frees.

questions.question: What does 'register' keyword suggest?
questions.hint: Store variable in a CPU register.

questions.question: What is 'restrict' keyword?
questions.hint: Tells compiler a pointer is the only way to access that memory.

questions.question: What is 'translation unit'?
questions.hint: A source file plus all included headers.

questions.question: What is 'integer promotion'?
questions.hint: Converting smaller integer types to int during evaluation.

questions.question: What is 'variadic function'?
questions.hint: A function that takes a variable number of arguments.

questions.question: What is 'interning' in some contexts?
questions.hint: Storing only one copy of each distinct string.

questions.question: What is 'buffer overflow'?
questions.hint: Writing past the end of an allocated buffer.

questions.question: What is 'masking'?
questions.hint: Using bitwise operators to isolate specific bits.

questions.question: What is 'alignment'?
questions.hint: Requirement that data be stored at addresses multiple of some value.

questions.question: What is 'linkage'?
questions.hint: Property that determines visibility across files.

questions.question: What is a 'function pointer'?
questions.hint: A pointer that stores the entry point of a function.

questions.question: What does 'extern' do?
questions.hint: Declares a variable defined in another file.

questions.question: What is 'DMA' in general computer context?
questions.cipherText: What is 'DMA' in general computer context?

questions.question: What is 'pointer arithmetic'?
questions.hint: Adding/subtracting from pointers based on type size.

questions.question: What is 'comma operator'?
questions.hint: Evaluates expressions and returns the last one.

questions.question: What is 'scope'?
questions.hint: Region where a variable is visible.

questions.question: What is 'recursion depth'?
questions.hint: Maximum number of nested function calls.

questions.question: What is 'pointer to constant'?
questions.hint: Value cannot be changed through pointer.

questions.question: What is 'side effect'?
questions.hint: Change in state outside of the returned value.

questions.question: What is 'int (*p)[5]'?
questions.hint: Pointer to an array of 5 integers.

questions.question: What is 'inline' keyword?
questions.hint: Suggests to compiler to expand function at call site.

questions.question: What is 'PTRDIFF_T'?
questions.hint: Signed integer type for pointer difference.

questions.question: What is 'page fault'?
questions.hint: Accessing a page not in RAM.

questions.question: What is 'void pointer arithmetic'?
questions.hint: It's illegal in standard C but allowed in GCC as 1 byte.

questions.question: What is 'segmentation'?
questions.hint: Division of memory into logical segments.

questions.question: What is 'tail recursion'?
questions.hint: Recursive call is the last action.

questions.question: What is 'static inline'?
questions.hint: Common in headers to avoid duplicate symbols.

questions.hint: Code injection technique that might destroy your database.

questions.hint: Linear collection of data elements where each element points to the next.

questions.hint: Device that forwards data packets between computer networks.

questions.hint: Device that connects devices together on a computer network.

questions.hint: Common connection point for devices in a network.

questions.hint: Wireless technology standard for exchanging data over short distances.

questions.hint: Proprietary operating system developed by Microsoft.

questions.hint: Proprietary operating system developed by Apple.

questions.hint: Mobile operating system based on a modified version of the Linux kernel.

questions.question: What is 'iOS'?
questions.hint: Mobile operating system developed by Apple.

questions.question: What is a 'tree'?
questions.hint: Hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes.

questions.question: What is a 'binary tree'?
questions.hint: Tree data structure in which each node has at most two children.

questions.question: What is a 'graph'?
questions.hint: Data structure consisting of vertices and edges.

questions.question: What is 'Hashing'?
questions.hint: Process of converting a key into another value.

questions.question: What is 'Sharding'?
questions.hint: Database partitioning that separates very large databases into smaller parts.

questions.question: What is 'Replication'?
questions.hint: Process of sharing information so as to ensure consistency between redundant resources.

questions.question: What is 'Data Warehouse'?
questions.hint: Central repository of integrated data from one or more disparate sources.

questions.question: What is 'Context Switching'?
questions.hint: Storing and restoring the state of a CPU.

questions.question: What is 'Thrashing'?
questions.hint: When the system spends more time paging than executing.

questions.question: What is a 'Semaphore'?
questions.hint: Variable or abstract data type used to control access to a common resource.

questions.question: What is 'Preemption'?
questions.hint: Act of temporarily interrupting a task being carried out by a computer system.

questions.question: What is 'Dynamic Programming'?
questions.hint: Method for solving a complex problem by breaking it down into a collection of simpler subproblems.

questions.question: What is 'Greedy Algorithm'?
questions.hint: Algorithm that makes the locally optimal choice at each stage.

questions.question: What is 'Dijkstra's Algorithm'?
questions.hint: Algorithm for finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph.

questions.question: What is 'A* Search'?
questions.hint: Graph traversal and path search algorithm.

questions.question: What is 'Zero Trust'?
questions.hint: Security model that requires all users to be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated.

questions.question: What is 'Salt' in hashing?
questions.hint: Random data used as an additional input to a one-way function.

questions.question: What is 'Serverless Computing'?
questions.hint: Cloud-computing execution model in which the cloud provider runs the server.

questions.question: What is 'Docker'?
questions.hint: Set of platform as a service products that use OS-level virtualization to deliver software in packages.

questions.question: What is 'Deep Learning'?
questions.hint: Part of a broader family of machine learning methods based on artificial neural networks.

questions.question: What is 'Supervised Learning'?
questions.hint: Machine learning task of learning a function that maps an input to an output based on example input-output pairs.

questions.question: What is 'Unsupervised Learning'?
questions.hint: Type of algorithm that learns patterns from untagged data.

questions.question: What is 'Reinforcement Learning'?
questions.hint: Area of machine learning concerned with how intelligent agents ought to take actions in an environment.

questions.question: What is a 'Parser'?
questions.hint: Software component that takes input data and builds a data structure.

questions.question: What is 'Intermediate Code'?
questions.hint: Code used by the compiler between source and target.

questions.question: What is 'Symbol Table'?
questions.hint: Data structure used by a language translator.

questions.question: What is 'Code Optimization'?
questions.hint: Process of modifying a software system to make some aspect of it work more efficiently.

questions.question: What is 'Microservices'?
questions.hint: Architectural style that structures an application as a collection of services.

questions.question: What is 'Load Balancing'?
questions.hint: Process of distributing network traffic across multiple servers.

questions.question: What is 'Consensus Algorithm'?
questions.hint: Process used to achieve agreement on a single data value among distributed processes or systems.

questions.question: What is 'Scalability'?
questions.hint: Property of a system to handle a growing amount of work.

questions.question: What is 'SOLID' principles?
questions.hint: Five design principles intended to make software designs more understandable, flexible, and maintainable.

questions.question: What is 'Design Pattern'?
questions.hint: General reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem within a given context in software design.

questions.question: What is 'Refactoring'?
questions.hint: Process of restructuring existing computer code.

questions.question: What is 'Unit Testing'?
questions.hint: Software testing method by which individual units of source code are tested.

questions.question: What is 'P vs NP'?
questions.hint: Major unsolved problem in computer science.

questions.question: What is 'Halting Problem'?
questions.hint: Problem of determining whether a program will finish running or continue to run forever.

questions.question: What is 'Big O Notation'?
questions.hint: Mathematical notation that describes the limiting behavior of a function.

questions.question: What is 'Chomsky Hierarchy'?
questions.hint: Containment hierarchy of classes of formal grammars.

questions.question: What is 'Subnetting'?
questions.hint: Strategy used to partition a single physical network into more than one smaller logical sub-networks.

questions.question: What is 'BGP Hijacking'?
questions.hint: Illegitimate takeover of groups of IP addresses.

questions.question: What is 'B-Tree'?
questions.hint: Self-balancing tree data structure that maintains sorted data.

questions.question: What is 'WAL' (Write-Ahead Logging)?
questions.hint: Family of techniques for providing atomicity and durability.

questions.question: What is 'Isolation Level'?
questions.hint: Degree to which a transaction must be isolated from data modifications made by other transactions.

questions.question: What is 'Deadlock Detection'?
questions.hint: Process of determining whether a deadlock exists.

questions.question: What is 'Query Optimizer'?
questions.hint: Component of a DBMS that attempts to determine the most efficient way to execute a given query.

questions.question: What is 'Slab Allocation'?
questions.hint: Memory management mechanism for efficient allocation of objects.

questions.question: What is 'Inverted Page Table'?
questions.hint: Page table structure where there is one entry for each real page of memory.

questions.question: What is 'Dirty Bit'?
questions.hint: Flag that indicates whether a block of memory has been modified.

questions.question: What is 'Bellman-Ford'?
questions.hint: Algorithm that computes shortest paths from a single source vertex to all of the other vertices in a weighted digraph.

questions.question: What is 'Floyd-Warshall'?
questions.hint: Algorithm for finding shortest paths in a weighted graph with positive or negative edge weights.

questions.question: What is 'Topological Sort'?
questions.hint: Linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge uv, vertex u comes before v.

questions.question: What is 'SCC' (Strongly Connected Components)?
questions.hint: Maximal subgraphs where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex.
